perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time on prior written notice to the other.
perturbation.changed_text: Aduro may terminate this Agreement at any time.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the Consultant's right to terminate the agreement, which is a legally required aspect of contract law. This creates an imbalance in the agreement, potentially violating California's implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing under California Law
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text allows Aduro to terminate the agreement at any time without affording the consultant the same right. This contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, which requires that parties act honestly and fairly in the performance and enforcement of the contract. Removing the consultant's termination right introduces a potential for abuse by Aduro and violates this implied covenant.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [1101 - 1310] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation [1201 - 1206] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 1201. (a) Unless the context otherwise requires, words or phrases defined in this section, or in the additional definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof, have the meanings stated. (b) Subject to definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof: (1) “Action,” in the sense of a judicial proceeding, includes recoupment, counterclaim, setoff, suit in equity, and any other proceeding in which rights are determined. (2) “Aggrieved party” means a party entitled to pursue a remedy. (3) “Agreement,” as distinguished from “contract,” means the bargain of the parties in fact, as found in their language or inferred from other circumstances, including course of performance, course of dealing, or usage of trade as provided in Section 1303. (4) “Bank” means a person engaged in the business of banking, and includes a savings bank, savings and loan association, credit union, and trust company. (5) “Bearer” means a person in possessio
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the consultant's right to terminate the agreement, an essential element under contract law. This constitutes an Omission Legal Contradiction because it omits a legally mandated aspect of fair dealing, creating an imbalance. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Agreement' broadly, but the modified agreement fails to ensure both parties have equivalent rights, potentially violating good faith as explained in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Aduro shall pay Consultant any amounts due that are not reasonably disputed by Aduro, by check or direct bank deposit, within thirty days after receiving the invoice.
perturbation.changed_text: Aduro shall pay Consultant any amounts due that are not reasonably disputed by Aduro.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the payment method and timeframe, creating potential issues under California law regarding timely payment for services rendered. This violates California Labor Code sections pertaining to prompt payment of wages, even to independent contractors.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the specific payment timeframe (within thirty days) and method, the modified contract creates ambiguity and allows Aduro to potentially delay or avoid payment. California Labor Code Section 2750.5 addresses independent contractor status and implications for wage payment standards. Although the consultant is an independent contractor, eliminating the specific timeframe creates a situation where Aduro might violate the spirit of prompt payment laws, making the contract legally questionable under California labor regulations and susceptible to challenges that the consultant is an employee.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, LAB 2750.5 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Labor Code - LAB DIVISION 3. EMPLOYMENT RELATIONS [2700 - 3122.4] ( Division 3 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 2. Employer and Employee [2750 - 2930] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 1. The Contract of Employment [2750 - 2755] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 2750.5. There is a rebuttable presumption affecting the burden of proof that a worker performing services for which a license is required pursuant to Chapter 9 (commencing with Section 7000) of Division 3 of the Business and Professions Code, or who is performing such services for a person who is required to obtain such a license is an employee rather than an independent contractor. Proof of independent contractor status includes satisfactory proof of these factors: (a) That the individual has the right to control and discretion as to the manner of performance of the contract for services in that the result of the work and not the means by which it is accomplished is the primary factor bargained for. (b) That the individual is customarily engaged in an independently established business. (c) That the individual’s independent contractor status is bona fide and not a subterfuge to avoid employee status. A bona fide independent contractor status is further evidenced by t
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the specific payment timeframe (within thirty days) and method (check or direct bank deposit) that were present in the original contract. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both suggest that this removal creates a potential contradiction with California labor regulations regarding prompt payment, even for independent contractors. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically Labor Code - LAB DIVISION 3. EMPLOYMENT RELATIONS [2700 - 3122.4], suggests the state regulates employment relations, and section 2750.5 addresses independent contractor status, providing a basis for the argument that the removal of the payment timeframe and method could create a situation where Aduro might violate the spirit of prompt payment laws.

perturbation.original_text: Consultant represents as follows: (a) Consultant is not subject to any other agreement that Consultant will violate by signing this Agreement; (b) Consultant has and shall continue to have the knowledge, experience, qualifications and required skill to perform, and shall perform, the Services in a professional manner; (c) Consultant to perform the Services in accordance with all Applicable Law; and (d) During the term of this Agreement, Consultant will not, directly or indirectly (whether for compensation or without compensation) engage in or provide consulting services, or enter into any agreement either written or oral, that would present a material conflict with any of the provisions of this Agreement, or would preclude Consultant from complying with the terms and conditions hereof. If during the term of this Agreement any situation or circumstance arises that might reasonably be expected to present a conflict of interest, or if Consultant might be unable to render Services or otherwise participate in such work without risk of breaching an obligation of confidentiality to another party, Consultant will promptly advise the Company's General Counsel of the situation and Company and Consultant shall, in good faith, attempt to resolve any such conflicts(s). If requested by the Company's General Counsel, Consultant will recuse herself from providing Services for the duration of the conflict.
perturbation.changed_text: Consultant represents as follows: (a) Consultant is not subject to any other agreement that Consultant will violate by signing this Agreement; (b) Consultant has and shall continue to have the knowledge, experience, qualifications and required skill to perform, and shall perform, the Services in a professional manner; (c) Consultant to perform the Services in accordance with all Applicable Law.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause concerning conflicts of interest and the requirement to inform the Company's General Counsel, the contract fails to adequately address potential ethical and legal conflicts that might arise during the consultancy. This omission weakens the company's protection against conflicts of interest, potentially violating California Business and Professions Code sections related to fair business practices and fiduciary responsibilities.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 17200 prohibits unfair competition. By omitting the obligation to disclose and resolve conflicts of interest, the modified contract potentially enables Consultant to engage in activities that are unfair to Aduro, to Aduro's business relationships, or to other third parties, thus weakening ethical business practices and potentially breaking compliance with California state rules.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 17200 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 5. Enforcement [17200 - 17210] ( Chapter 5 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 299. ) 17200. As used in this chapter, unfair competition shall mean and include any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice and unfair, deceptive, untrue or misleading advertising and any act prohibited by Chapter 1 (commencing with Section 17500) of Part 3 of Division 7 of the Business and Professions Code. (Amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 430, Sec. 2. Effective January 1, 1993.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract omits the consultant's obligation to disclose and resolve conflicts of interest, a significant departure from the original agreement. This omission creates a legal loophole by removing a mechanism to prevent unfair business practices, which is a violation of California Business and Professions Code Section 17200. The scraped snippet defines unfair competition broadly as 'any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice'. By removing the conflict of interest clause, the modified contract increases the risk of such unfair practices occurring, contradicting the intent of the law. This aligns with the 'Omission Legal Contradiction' definition, as it removes essential information that protects against potential legal and ethical conflicts.
